---
title: "Compgenerics study 9 (features) exploratory study"
author: Marianna Zhang
date: 2025-10-17
output: 
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
  word_document:
    toc: true
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
# load libraries
library(here)       # navigating directories

library(janitor)    # cleaning data
library(scales)     # percent conversion
library(gt)         # table formatting

library(tidyverse)  # data wrangling & visualization
library(grid)       # custom plotting

library(glmmTMB)    # beta mixed effects models
library(car)        # anova type II-III
library(emmeans)    # pairwise comparisons

library(reticulate)

# don't show code or messages in knitted doc
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE)

knitr::opts_template$set(
  plot = list(
    echo = FALSE,
    results = TRUE),
  analysis = list(
    echo = TRUE,
    results = FALSE)
)

# prevent scientific notation for numerals
options(scipen = 999)

# format p-values for reporting
format_p <- function(p) { 
  if (p < .001) {
    return("< .001")
  } else {
    return(paste0("= ", round(p, digits = 3)))
  }
}
```

```{r vis-theme, include = FALSE}
# set visualization theme
theme_set(
  ggthemes::theme_few() +
    theme(legend.title = element_text(size = 14),
          legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
          plot.caption = element_text(size = 8),
          text = element_text(size = 16), # large text size for presentation figures
          axis.ticks.x = element_blank()) # remove x-axis ticks
)

# map test feature names
test_feature_names = c(
  "snore_sleep" = "snore when they sleep",
  "can_snap_toes" = "can snap with their toes",
  "cold_hands_feet" = "have cold hands and feet",
  "can_wiggle_ears" = "can wiggle their ears",
  "can_roll_tongue" = "can roll their tongue",
  
  "can_eat_spicy" = "can eat spicy food",
  "breakfast_late" = "eat breakfast very late",
  "like_juice_pulp" = "like juice with pulp",
  "five_meals_day" = "eat five meals a day",
  "pepper_on_all" = "put pepper on all their foods",
  
  "collect_rocks" = "like to collect rocks",
  "like_highfive" = "like to give high-fives",
  "read_books" = "like to read books",
  "cry_easily" = "cry easily",
  "like_to_dance" = "like to dance"
)

# set visualization colors
condition_colors = c(
    "physical" = "#8B0000",
    "diet" = "#006400",
    "personality" = "#000080",
    "heterogeneous" = "black"
)

feature_type_colors = c(
    "physical" = "#FF7F7F",
    "diet" = "#3CB371",
    "personality" = "#4682B4"
)

match_colors = c(
  "match" = "purple",
  "heterogeneous" = "gray50",
  "mismatch" = "gray70"
)

```

# Summary

Study 9 was an exploratory study to explore how generic statements about certain features affect adults' generalization across similar versus different features. 

In this study, adults (n = 397, 98-100/condition) heard 15 generics about similar features of Zarpies (i.e., physical, diet and food preferences, or personality and related behaviors) or 15 generics about heterogeneous features of Zarpies (i.e., a fixed subset of 5 generics from each of the aforementioned clusters). Adults then completed an inductive potential task: in each trial, they observed a Zarpie with a novel feature, and rated its prevalence among Zarpies (0-100\%). Trials involved 5 novel features from each cluster (i.e., 5 novel physical, diet, and personality features), comprising 15 novel features in total. 

We were thinking:

1. If adults learn **feature-specific coherence** based on the features in the generics they heard, then adults who hear generics about certain features should generalize more within that feature type than across feature types. In other words, the physical condition may lead to higher prevalence estimates for novel physical features, compared to diet or personality conditions, etc. 

2. Also, hearing generics about a broad range of features may lead to **higher overall coherence** than hearing generics about a specific set of features. In other words, the heterogeneous condition may lead to higher overall prevalence estimates than the specific feature conditions.

What we found:

1. We found mixed evidence for feature-specific coherence.

  * In general, conditions where participants heard the generics about the same type of feature led to higher prevalence for test features in the same type (see [By test feature type match]). 
  
  * However, when looking at specific conditions, only the physical condition led to higher prevalence estimates for the features of the same type compared to other single-type conditions. 

2. We did not find evidence for hearing heterogenous generics leading to higher coherence. 

  * The heterogeneous condition did not lead to higher overall prevalence estimates than the specific feature conditions (see [Overall]).
  
3. We also asked participants to freeform describe what characterizes Zarpies as a group, and found descriptions that generally matched their conditions, although physical responses remained rare, even in the physical condition where they were most common.  


# Methods

```{r data-import}
# import Qualtrics data
data_raw_headers <- 
  read_csv(here("data", "study9.csv"), 
           n_max = 2) 
  
data_raw <- 
  read_csv(here("data", "study9.csv"),
           col_names = data_raw_headers %>% names(),
           skip = 3) 

# import demographic data from Prolific
data_dem_raw <-
  read_csv(here("data", "study9_dem.csv"))
```

```{r data-cleaning}
data_dem <- data_dem_raw %>% 
  # clean column names
  clean_names() 

data_all <- data_raw %>% 
  
  # join with demographic data
  left_join(data_dem, 
            by = "hashed_id") %>% 
  
  # clean column names
  rename_with(~str_remove(., '_1')) %>% 
  rename(test_features_order = InductionTask_DO) %>% 
  
  # join self-describe demographic columns
  mutate(gender = coalesce(gender_4_TEXT, gender),
         race = coalesce(race5_TEXT, race),
         training_features_order = coalesce(physicalfeatures_DO, dietfeatures_DO,
                                            personalityfeatures_DO, heterogeneousfeatures_DO)) %>% 
  
  # remove extraneous columns
  select(-c(StartDate:pledge,
            gender_4_TEXT, race5_TEXT,
            sound_check_DO, FL2_DO, check_task_DO,
            physicalfeatures_DO, dietfeatures_DO, personalityfeatures_DO, heterogeneousfeatures_DO,
            issues:about,
            submission_id:archived_at,
            completion_code
  )) %>% 
  
  # reorder columns
  relocate(c(condition, training_features_order),
           .after = sound_check) %>% 
  
  # add participant ID column
  mutate(participant = row_number(),
         .before = everything()) %>% 
  
  # convert prevalence to 0-1, trim endpoints
  mutate(across(diet_can_eat_spicy:phys_snore_sleep, \(x) as.numeric(x)/100)) %>% 
  mutate(across(diet_can_eat_spicy:phys_snore_sleep, \(x) case_when(
    x == 0 ~ 0.01,
    x == 1 ~ 0.99,
    TRUE ~ x))) %>%
  
  # fix column types, levels
  mutate(condition = condition %>% 
           factor(levels = c("physical", 
                             "diet",
                             "personality",
                             "heterogeneous")),
         age = age %>% as.numeric(),
         education = education %>% factor(
           levels = c(
             "Less than high school", 
             "High school/GED", 
             "Some college", 
             "Bachelor's (B.A., B.S.)", 
             "Master's (M.A., M.S.)", 
             "Doctoral (Ph.D., J.D., M.D.)", 
             "Prefer not to specify"))) 
```

## Participants

```{r sample}
# record total collected, failures on each check question
sample <- data_all %>% 
  summarize(n_collect = n(),
            sound_check = sum(sound_check != "bird" | is.na(sound_check)),
            attn_check = sum(attn_check != 100 | is.na(attn_check)),
            AI = sum(AI != "No" | is.na(AI)),
            check_task = sum(check_task != "Answering questions about a fictional group of people"))

# exclude participants
data <- data_all %>%
  filter(
    sound_check == "bird",
    attn_check == 100,
    AI == "No",
    check_task == "Answering questions about a fictional group of people") %>% 
  select(-c(sound_check, attn_check, AI, check_task))

# record number of participants in actual sample after exclusions, exclusion rate
sample <- sample %>% 
  mutate(n = nrow(data),
         n_excl = n_collect - n,
         excl_rate = n_excl/n_collect)
```

Data was collected from `r sample$n_collect` adults (n = 98-100/condition) via Prolific on Thursday-Friday 10/16-10/17/2025. Participants required to be in the United States, fluent in English, and having not participated in prior studies under this protocol. Participants were paid $1.75 for an estimated 7 minute task. Participants were requested to particpate via desktop.

```{r sample-condition}
# Record number of participants in actual sample per condition
sample_condition <- data %>% 
  group_by(condition) %>% 
  summarize(n = length(unique(participant)))

sample_condition %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  gt()
```

### Exclusion criteria

We recruited 400 participants, of whom `r sample$n_excl` participants (`r label_percent(0.1) (sample$excl_rate)` of all participants) were excluded for meeting at least 1 of the following exclusion criteria:

* failing the sound check (i.e., did not select "bird" as the sound heard during the sound check video) (n = `r sample$sound_check` participants)

* failing the attention check (i.e., did not select 100% on slider when asked to during induction task) (n = `r sample$attn_check` participants)

* admitting to use of AI after being explicitly informed use was prohibited (n = `r sample$AI` participants)

* failing the task check (n = `r sample$check_task` participants)

### Demographics

We used the Prolific representative sample feature to recruit a sample representative of the US based on Census data on sex, age, and ethnicity (Simplified US Census). 

```{r dem-age}
## Age
dem_age <- data %>%
  summarize(mean = mean(age),
            sd = sd(age),
            n = n())

data %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = age)) +
  geom_density() +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(18, 100))

dem_age %>%
  gt() %>% 
  tab_header("age") %>% 
  fmt_number(
    columns = mean:sd,
    decimals = 2,
    use_seps = FALSE
  )
```
* The sample skewed young in age. 

```{r dem-gender}
## Gender
dem_gender <- data %>%
  count(gender, sort = TRUE) %>% 
  mutate(prop = n/sample$n)

dem_gender %>% 
  mutate(prop = label_percent(0.1) (prop)) %>% 
  gt() 
```

* The sample reflected the diversity of the gender identities in the US.

[(`r label_percent() (dem_gender %>% filter(gender == "Male") %>% pull(prop))` male, `r label_percent() (dem_gender %>% filter(gender == "Female") %>% pull(prop))` female, `r label_percent() (dem_gender %>% filter(gender == "Non-binary") %>% pull(prop))` non-binary, `r label_percent() (dem_gender %>% filter(gender == "Prefer not to specify") %>% pull(prop))` prefer not to specify)]: # 

```{r dem-race}
## Race
dem_race <- data %>%
  count(race, sort = TRUE) %>% 
  mutate(prop = n/sample$n)

dem_race_lumped <- data %>%
  mutate(race_lumped = fct_lump_prop(race, 0.025)) %>%
  count(race_lumped, sort = TRUE) %>% 
  mutate(prop = n/sample$n)

dem_race %>% 
  mutate(prop = label_percent(0.1) (prop)) %>% 
  gt() 
```

* The sample was also racially diverse.

[`r label_percent() (dem_race %>% filter(race == "White, Caucasian, or European American") %>% pull(prop))` White, Caucasian or European American; `r label_percent() (dem_race %>% filter(race == "Black or African American") %>% pull(prop))` Black or African American, `r label_percent() (dem_race %>% filter(race == "Hispanic or Latino/a") %>% pull(prop))` Hispanic or Latino/a, `r label_percent() (dem_race %>% filter(race == "East Asian") %>% pull(prop))` East Asian, `r label_percent() (dem_race %>% filter(race == "South or Southeast Asian") %>% pull(prop))` South or Southeast Asian.]: #

```{r dem-education}
## Education
dem_education <- data %>%
  count(education) %>% 
  mutate(prop = n/sample$n)

dem_education %>% 
  mutate(prop = label_percent(0.1) (prop)) %>% 
  gt() 
```

* The sample was about evenly split on college completion. 

## Procedure

This study was administered as a [Qualtrics survey](https://nyu.qualtrics.com/survey-builder/SV_5cIiWqnmHxbE0Hc/edit?SurveyID=SV_5cIiWqnmHxbE0Hc), and approved by the NYU IRB (IRB-FY2023-6812).

After providing their consent, participants completed a captcha, pledge not to use AI, and sound check. Participants then completed:

1. **Training phase**: participants heard 15 generic statements in random order. Participants were randomly assigned to one of 4 conditions:

  * Diet condition - 15 generic statements about Zarpies' diet and food preferences
  * Physical condition - 15 generic statements about Zarpies' physical features
  * Personality condition - 15 generic statements about Zarpies' personality and related behaviors
  * Heterogeneous condition - a fixed subset of 5 generic statements from each of the 3 feature types above (diet, physical, personality)

2. **Test phase (induction task)**: Participants completed an induction task where they imagined seeing a Zarpie with a novel feature, and estimated the prevalence of that feature among Zarpies using a slider from 0 to 100 (initialized at 0). All participants completed the same 15 trials, with order of trials randomized:

  * 5 physical features
  * 5 diet features
  * 5 personality features
  
3. **Test phase (group characterization)**: Participants were then asked to respond to a freeform question asking: "What do you think characterizes Zarpies as a group?" Responses were loosely coded blind to condition by Marianna. 

Participants then completed a few task completion questions, demographics, and were debriefed. 


## Data processing

Prevalence judgments were converted to a scale from 0 to 1, with 0 and 1 values trimmed to 0.01 and 0.99 to support a beta regression, since a uniform beta distribution does not include its endpoints of 0 and 1. 

# Computational modeling

To get a sense of feature space, we embed all the features (training and test features), as they appear in generic statements, using a sentence transformer (MiniLM). We then use PCA to reduce that multi-dimensional space down to a 2 dimensional feature space. The code doing this and generating these plots is in Python, in the project folder under "Model".

## Stimuli{.tabset}

### All conditions
![All training features plus test features in 2D feature space based on embeddings from a sentence embedding model.](/Users/MariannaZhang/Library/CloudStorage/Box-Box/NYU/projects/compgenerics/model/figs/study 9/stimuli.png)

### Physical condition
![Physical training features (darker circles) plus test features (lighter diamonds) in 2D feature space based on embeddings from a sentence embedding model.](/Users/MariannaZhang/Library/CloudStorage/Box-Box/NYU/projects/compgenerics/model/figs/study 9/stimuli_physical.png)

### Diet condition
![Diet training features (darker circles) plus test features (lighter diamonds) in 2D feature space based on embeddings from a sentence embedding model.](/Users/MariannaZhang/Library/CloudStorage/Box-Box/NYU/projects/compgenerics/model/figs/study 9/stimuli_diet.png)

### Personality condition
![Personality training features (darker circles) plus test features (lighter diamonds) in 2D feature space based on embeddings from a sentence embedding model.](/Users/MariannaZhang/Library/CloudStorage/Box-Box/NYU/projects/compgenerics/model/figs/study 9/stimuli_personality.png)


## Predicted concepts{.tabset}

The training conditions are all generics, which indicate what features are known to be kind-linked. Based on what features are known to be kind-linked, our feature-specific model tries to fit a multivariate Gaussian function (3D) over feature space (2D). This multivariate Gaussian function can be thought of a "kind concept", and is centered on a mean and has a covariance matrix defining its spread. For example, if the Gaussian is centered over physical features, it will have stronger generalization to other physical features, and weaker generalization to more distant features in feature space. 

We can then use the model to make predictions about novel test features based on their embedding location in feature space and the Gaussian concept:

1. The Gaussian provides a probability over "kind scores" for each test feature. 

2. A test feature's "kind score" in turn is entered into a beta distribution which provides a probability over the likelihood of the test feature being kind-linked. 

3. The likelihood of the test feature being kind-linked then sets a Bernoulli distribution, which determines whether the test feature is in fact kind-linked. 

4. Note that we have yet to link this model to prevalence judgments (or embed it in the rational speech acts framework). 

It's hard to visualize a 3D Gaussian over a 2D space, so the below visualizations use ellipses to depict cross-sections from sampled Gaussians, to show approximately what the expected Gaussian might look like after each training condition.  


### Physical condition

![Example kind concepts after the physical condition.](/Users/MariannaZhang/Library/CloudStorage/Box-Box/NYU/projects/compgenerics/model/figs/study 9/model_physical.png)

### Diet condition

![Example kind concepts after the diet condition.](/Users/MariannaZhang/Library/CloudStorage/Box-Box/NYU/projects/compgenerics/model/figs/study 9/model_diet.png)


### Personality condition

![Example kind concepts after the personality condition.](/Users/MariannaZhang/Library/CloudStorage/Box-Box/NYU/projects/compgenerics/model/figs/study 9/model_personality.png)

### Heterogeneous condition

![Example kind concepts after the heterogeneous condition.](/Users/MariannaZhang/Library/CloudStorage/Box-Box/NYU/projects/compgenerics/model/figs/study 9/model_heterogeneous.png)


```{r import-cosine-similarity}
# get avg cosine similarity for each test feature and condition
test_features_avg_cosine_similarities_by_condition <-
  read_csv(here("model", "scratch", "study 9", "study9_test_features_avg_cosine_similarities_by_condition.csv")) %>% 
  rename("condition" = "...1") %>% 
  mutate(condition = condition %>% 
           str_replace_all("diet_preferences", "diet") %>%
           str_replace_all("personality_behaviors", "personality") %>%
           factor(levels = c("physical",
                             "diet",
                             "personality",
                             "heterogeneous"))) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = -condition,
               names_to = "test_feature",
               values_to = "cosine_similarity_avg")

# get max cosine similarity for each test feature and condition
test_features_max_cosine_similarities_by_condition <-
  read_csv(here("model", "scratch", "study 9", "study9_test_features_max_cosine_similarities_by_condition.csv")) %>% 
  rename("condition" = "...1") %>% 
  mutate(condition = condition %>% 
           str_replace_all("diet_preferences", "diet") %>%
           str_replace_all("personality_behaviors", "personality") %>%
           factor(levels = c("physical",
                             "diet",
                             "personality",
                             "heterogeneous"))) %>% 
  pivot_longer(cols = -condition,
               names_to = "test_feature",
               values_to = "cosine_similarity_max")
```

```{r tidy-data}
# tidy data
data_tidy <- data %>% 
  
  # tidy data
  pivot_longer(cols = diet_can_eat_spicy:phys_snore_sleep,
               names_to = "test_feature_type_name",
               values_to = "prevalence") %>% 
  
  # extract the order of test features (disregarding attention check)
  mutate(test_feature_order = map2_int(test_feature_type_name, test_features_order,
                                       function(feat, order){
                                         order_split <- str_split_1(order, '\\|') %>% # split test_features_order by |
                                           discard(\(x) x == "attn_check") # discard attention check
                                         match(feat, order_split)[1] # get first matching index
                                       })) %>%
  select(-test_features_order) %>% 
  
  # separate test feature type and abbv name
  separate(test_feature_type_name,
           into = c("test_feature_type", "test_feature_abbv"),
           extra = "merge") %>% 
  
  # clean feature type
  mutate(test_feature_type = case_when(
    test_feature_type == "phys" ~ "physical",
    test_feature_type == "diet" ~ "diet",
    test_feature_type == "pers" ~ "personality",
  ) %>% 
    factor(levels = c("physical",
                      "diet",
                      "personality"))) %>% 
  
  # get full feature name
  mutate(test_feature = test_feature_names[test_feature_abbv]) %>%
  
  # add match column between training and test feature type
  mutate(condition_test_match = 
           case_when(
             condition == "physical" & test_feature_type == "physical" ~ "match",
             condition == "diet" & test_feature_type == "diet" ~ "match",
             condition == "personality" & test_feature_type == "personality" ~ "match",
             condition == "heterogeneous" ~ "heterogeneous",
             TRUE ~ "mismatch"
           ) %>% 
           factor(levels = c("match", "heterogeneous", "mismatch"))) %>% 
  
  # add contrast columns for testing contrasts
  mutate(contrast_physical = if_else(condition == "physical", 3/4, -1/4),
         .after = condition) %>% 
  
  # add cosine similarity
  left_join(test_features_avg_cosine_similarities_by_condition,
            by = c("condition", "test_feature")) %>% 
  left_join(test_features_max_cosine_similarities_by_condition,
            by = c("condition", "test_feature"))
```


# Primary results

## Induction task {.tabset}

Analyses of the induction task were logistic regressions unless otherwise specified, predicting prevalence (.01-.99) with participant and test feature as random intercepts. Test feature ("can snap with their toes", etc.) is technically nested within test feature type (physical, diet, personality), but since each test feature is unique to each test feature type, a model with the nesting term is analytically equivalent to the previous model, so the nesting term was omitted for simplicity of specification. 

### By test feature

We can look at how prevalence judgments vary by condition and individual test feature. 


```{r plot-induction-testfeature-condition}
# summary plot of prevalence by test feature type and condition
data_tidy %>% 
  
  # make plot
  ggplot(aes(y = prevalence,
             x = test_feature,
             color = condition)) + 
  
  # let x-axis be free
  
  facet_grid(cols = vars(test_feature_type),
             scales = "free_x") +
  
  # condition colors
  scale_color_manual(values = condition_colors) +
  
  # add data points
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.007,
              position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = .3,
                                              dodge.width = .6)) +
  
  # add confidence intervals + means
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
               geom = "linerange",
               position = position_dodge(width = .6),
               size = 1) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean,
               geom = "point",
               position = position_dodge(width = .6),
               size = 2) +
  
  # customize axes
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45,
                                   hjust = 1)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  scale_x_discrete(name = "test feature") +
  
  # add title
  ggtitle("Prevalence by test feature and condition")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "prevalence_by_testfeature_condition.png")
```


### By test feature type

We can look at how prevalence judgments vary by condition and test feature type (i.e., physical, diet, or personality). 

If the chosen clusters capture some systematicity in how people generalize, the physical condition should make the highest prevalence estimates for physical test features, the diet condition for the diet test features, and the personality condition for personality test features. This appears to be true for the physical and personality conditions, but not for the diet condition.  

```{r plot-induction-testfeaturetype-condition}
# summary plot of prevalence by test feature type and condition
data_tidy %>% 
  
  # make plot
  ggplot(aes(y = prevalence,
             x = test_feature_type,
             color = condition)) + 
  
  # condition colors
  scale_color_manual(values = condition_colors) +
  
  # add data points
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.007,
              position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = .3,
                                              dodge.width = .6)) +
  
  # add confidence intervals + means
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
               geom = "linerange",
               position = position_dodge(width = .6),
               size = 1) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean,
               geom = "point",
               position = position_dodge(width = .6),
               size = 2) +
  
  # customize axes
  scale_y_continuous(name = "prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  scale_x_discrete(name = "test feature type") +
  
  # add title
  ggtitle("Prevalence by test feature type and condition")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "prevalence_by_testfeaturetype_condition.png",
       height = 5,
       width = 7.5)
```

```{r plot-induction-condition-testfeaturetype}
# summary plot of prevalence by condition and test feature type
data_tidy %>% 
  
  # make plot
  ggplot(aes(y = prevalence,
             x = condition,
             color = test_feature_type)) + 
  
  # condition colors
  scale_color_manual(values = feature_type_colors) +
  
  # add data points
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.007,
              position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = .3,
                                              dodge.width = .6)) +
  
  # add confidence intervals + means
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
               geom = "linerange",
               position = position_dodge(width = .6),
               size = 1) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean,
               geom = "point",
               position = position_dodge(width = .6),
               size = 2) +
  
  # customize axes
  scale_y_continuous(name = "prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  
  # customize legend name
  labs(color = "test feature type") +
  
  # add title
  ggtitle("Prevalence by condition and test feature type")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "prevalence_by_condition_testfeaturetype.png",
       height = 5,
       width = 7.5)
```

```{r analysis-condition-testfeaturetype, echo = TRUE, include = TRUE}
# condition * test feature type
glmm_condition_testfeaturetype <-
  glmmTMB(prevalence ~ condition * test_feature_type + (1|participant) + (1|test_feature), 
          data = data_tidy, 
          family = beta_family(link = "logit"))

glmm_condition_testfeaturetype %>% 
  Anova()

glmm_condition_testfeaturetype %>% 
  emmeans(~ condition * test_feature_type) %>%
  contrast(method = "pairwise") %>%
  summary(adjust = "FDR")
```
Indeed, there is a significant interaction between condition and test feature type in an ANOVA conducted on a logistic regression with condition, test feature type, and their interaction as fixed effects, and with participant and test feature as random intercepts ($\chi$(6) = 86.17, *p* < .001). There is also a main effect of test feature type ($\chi$(2) = 6.62, *p* = .037) and a marginal effect of condition ($\chi$(3) = 6.49, *p* = .090). 

When rating the prevalence of physical features, the physical condition produced significantly higher prevalence estimates than the diet condition (FDR-corrected *z* = 4.26, *p* = .0014) or personality condition (*z* = 3.33, *p* = .011), but no different from the heterogeneous condition (*z* = 2.28, *p* = .11). 

When rating the prevalence of diet features, the diet condition did not produce different prevalence estimates than the physical condition (*z* = 0.88, *p* = .47), personality condition (*z* = 1.23, *p* = 0.36), or heterogeneous condition (*z* = 1.01, *p* = .43). 

When rating the prevalence of personality features, the personality condition produced only marginally higher prevalence estimates than the diet condition (*z* = 2.48, *p* = .072), and heterogeneous condition (*z* = 2.13, *p* = .11), and no different from the physical condition (*z* = 1.03, *p* = .43).


```{r analysis-condition-testfeaturetype-phys, echo = TRUE, include = TRUE}
# make contrast matrix for condition
C <- matrix(
  c(
    # physical  diet   pers    hetero
      1,       -1/3,  -1/3,   -1/3,    # Contrast 1: physical vs others
      0,        1,    -1,      0,      # Contrast 2: diet vs personality
      0,        1,     0,     -1,      # Contrast 3: diet vs heterogeneous
      1,        1,     1,      1       # Overall mean (intercept)
  ),
  nrow = 4,
  byrow = TRUE
)

# assign row names
rownames(C) <- levels(data_tidy$condition)

# apply and center columns
contrasts(data_tidy$condition) <- C[,1:3]  # first 3 rows are true contrasts


# condition * test feature type
glmm_condition_testfeaturetype_phys <-
  glmmTMB(prevalence ~ condition * test_feature_type + (1|participant) + (1|test_feature), 
          data = data_tidy, 
          family = beta_family(link = "logit"))

glmm_condition_testfeaturetype_phys %>% 
  summary()
```


### By test feature type match

Another way to look at the data is to code responses by whether the test feature type matched the training condition. If they match (e.g., diet condition responding to a diet test question), we can code that as a match, or if they mismatch (e.g., diet condition responding to a personality test question), we can code that as a mismatch. We can leave the heterogeneous condition as its own category, since it's a semi-match to everything. 

If the chosen clusters capture some systematicity in how people generalize, matches should result in higher prevalence estimates than mismatches. Indeed, that's what we find. 


```{r plot-induction-testfeature-condition-match}
# summary plot of prevalence by test feature type and condition

data_tidy %>% 
  
  # make plot
  ggplot(aes(y = prevalence,
             x = test_feature_type,
             group = condition,
             color = condition_test_match)) + 
  
  # condition colors
  scale_color_manual(values = match_colors) +
  
  # add data points
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.007,
              position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = .3,
                                              dodge.width = .6)) +
  
  # add confidence intervals + means
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
               geom = "linerange",
               position = position_dodge(width = .6),
               size = 1) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean,
               geom = "point",
               position = position_dodge(width = .6),
               size = 2) +
  
  # customize axes
  theme(legend.title = element_text(size = 14),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
        plot.caption = element_text(size = 8)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  scale_x_discrete(name = "test feature type") +
  
  # customize legend name
  labs(color = "condition and \ntest feature type") +
  
  # add title
  ggtitle("Prevalence by test feature type and condition")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "prevalence_by_testfeaturetype_condition_match.png",
       height = 5,
       width = 7.5)
```

```{r plot-induction-match}
# summary plot of prevalence by test feature type and condition
data_tidy %>% 
  
  # make plot
  ggplot(aes(y = prevalence,
             x = condition_test_match,
             color = condition_test_match)) +
  
  # color palette
  scale_color_manual(values = match_colors) +
  
  # add data points
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.007,
              position = position_jitter(width = .3)) +
  
  # add confidence intervals + means
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
               geom = "linerange",
               size = 1) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean,
               geom = "point",
               size = 2) +
  
  # customize axes
  theme(legend.title = element_text(size = 14),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
        plot.caption = element_text(size = 8)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  scale_x_discrete(name = "condition and test feature type match?") +
  
  # remove legend
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  
  # add title
  ggtitle("Prevalence by condition and test feature type match")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "prevalence_by_match.png",
       height = 5,
       width = 5.75)
```

```{r analysis-match, echo = TRUE, include = TRUE}
# condition
glmm_condition_test_match <-
  glmmTMB(prevalence ~ condition_test_match + (1|participant) + (1|test_feature), 
          data = data_tidy, 
          family = beta_family(link = "logit"))

glmm_condition_test_match %>% 
  Anova()

glmm_condition_test_match %>% 
  emmeans(~ condition_test_match) %>% 
  contrast(method = "pairwise") %>% 
  summary()
```
Indeed, there is a main effect of whether condition and test variables match (match, hetereogenous, or mismatch) on prevalence, in an ANOVA conducted on a logistic regression with match as a main effect, and with participant and test feature as random intercepts ($\chi$(2) = 74.03, *p* < .001). Post-hoc FDR-corrected pairwise comparisons reveal that the matching condition results in higher prevalence estimates of test features than the heterogeneous condition (*z* = 8.58, *p* < .001) or the mismatching conditions (*z* = 2.29, *p* = .034).


### By cosine similarity

Instead of grouping features into discrete types, we can also look at the distance (cosine similarity) between each individual test feature to the training features presented in each condition, in the multidimensional embedding space. 

For each test feature, we can calculate the average distance to training features in each condition, and see if that metric of cosine similarity predicts measures of prevalence. 

```{r analysis-cosine-similarity, opts.label='analysis'}
# average cosine similarity of the test feature, to the training features in that condition
glmm_cosine_similarity_avg <-
  glmmTMB(prevalence ~ cosine_similarity_avg + (1|participant) + (1|test_feature), 
          data = data_tidy, 
          family = beta_family(link = "logit"))

glmm_cosine_similarity_avg %>% 
  summary()
```
Indeed, there is a significant effect of **average cosine similarity** of the test feature to the various training features in the condition ($z$ = `r summary(glmm_cosine_similarity_avg)$coefficients$cond["cosine_similarity_avg", "z value"] %>% round(2)`, *p* `r summary(glmm_cosine_similarity_avg)$coefficients$cond["cosine_similarity_avg", "Pr(>|z|)"] %>% format_p()`), such that higher average cosine similarity predicts higher prevalence estimates, in a logistic model with random intercepts per participant and test feature. 


```{r plot-cosine-similarity-vs-prevalence, opts.label='plot'}
# avg cosine similarity vs prevalence
data_tidy %>% 
  group_by(condition, test_feature_type, test_feature) %>% 
  summarize(avg_cos_sim = mean(cosine_similarity_avg),
            avg_prevalence = mean(prevalence)) %>% 
  
  ggplot(aes(x = avg_cos_sim, y = avg_prevalence)) +
  
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "average cosine similarity to training features") + 
  labs(caption = "dots = each test feature (15 feature), within condition (3 conditions)")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       "prevalence_by_cosine_similarity.png")
```

```{r plot-cosine-similarity-vs-prevalence-condition, opts.label='plot'}
# cosine similarity vs prevalence by condition
data_tidy %>% 
  group_by(condition, test_feature_type, test_feature) %>% 
  summarize(avg_cos_sim = mean(cosine_similarity_avg),
            avg_prevalence = mean(prevalence)) %>% 
  
  ggplot(aes(x = avg_cos_sim, y = avg_prevalence, color = condition)) +
  
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm") +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "average cosine similarity to training features")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "prevalence_by_cosine_similarity_condition.png")
```

We can also focus on maximum cosine similarity, i.e., the distance from the test feature to the closest training feature in a given condition, and see if that metric predicts prevalence judgments. 

```{r analysis-cosine-similarity-max, opts.label='analysis'}
# max cosine similarity of the test feature, to the closest training features in that condition
glmm_cosine_similarity_max <-
  glmmTMB(prevalence ~ cosine_similarity_max + (1|participant) + (1|test_feature), 
          data = data_tidy, 
          family = beta_family(link = "logit"))

glmm_cosine_similarity_max %>% 
  summary()
```

Indeed, maximum cosine similarity is also a significant predictor of prevalence estimates ($z$ = `r summary(glmm_cosine_similarity_max)$coefficients$cond["cosine_similarity_max", "z value"] %>% round(2)`, *p* `r summary(glmm_cosine_similarity_max)$coefficients$cond["cosine_similarity_max", "Pr(>|z|)"] %>% format_p()`), such that higher maximum cosine similarity predicts higher prevalence estimates, in a logistic model with random intercepts per participant and test feature.

```{r analysis-cosine-similarity-avg-vs-max, opts.label='analysis'}
glmmTMB(prevalence ~ cosine_similarity_avg + cosine_similarity_max + (1|participant) + (1|test_feature), 
          data = data_tidy, 
          family = beta_family(link = "logit"))  %>% 
  summary()
```

However, when including both average and max cosine similarity as predictors, **only average cosine similarity remains a significant predictor of prevalence estimates**, suggesting that people are integrating over all training features in the condition, rather than just attending to the most similar training feature.



### Overall

We can look at prevalence estimates overall. If the heterogeneous condition leads to the highest overall coherence, we should see the highest prevalence estimates in that condition overall. However, that's not what we find. 

```{r plot-induction-condition}
# summary plot of prevalence by condition
data_tidy %>% 
  
  # make plot
  ggplot(aes(y = prevalence,
             x = condition,
             color = condition)) + 
  
  # condition colors
  scale_color_manual(values = condition_colors) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = condition_colors) +
  
  # add data points
  geom_point(alpha = 0.005,
              position = position_jitterdodge(jitter.width = .3,
                                              dodge.width = 1)) +
  
  # add confidence intervals + means
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
               geom = "linerange",
               position = position_dodge(width = 1),
               size = 1) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean,
               geom = "point",
               position = position_dodge(width = 1),
               size = 2) +
  
  # customize axes
  theme(legend.title = element_text(size = 14),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
        plot.caption = element_text(size = 8)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  
  # remove legend
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  
  # add title
  ggtitle("Prevalence by condition")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "prevalence_by_condition.png",
       height = 5,
       width = 5)
```

```{r analysis-condition, echo = TRUE, include = TRUE}
# condition * test feature type
glmm_condition <-
  glmmTMB(prevalence ~ condition + (1|participant) + (1|test_feature_type),
          data = data_tidy, 
          family = beta_family(link = "logit"))

glmm_condition %>% 
  Anova()

glmm_condition %>% 
  emmeans("condition") %>%
  contrast(method = "pairwise") %>%
  summary(adjust = "FDR")
```

There is only a marginal effect of condition on prevalence ($\chi$(3) = 6.48, *p* = .091). Post-hoc FDR-corrected pairwise comparisons reveal no significant differences between any conditions (*p*s > .10).

### By test feature, vs model

We can get the model's predictions and compare those to people's ratings of prevalence. For now, we get the model's "kind score" for each test feature, which is a measure of the expected value of the Gaussian function at that location in feature space. 

```{r model-scores}
# get model scores for each feature
model_test_feature_scores <-
  read_csv(here("model", "scratch", "study 9", "study9_model_test_feature_scores.csv")) %>% 
  
  # clean and order condition, feature type labels
  mutate(condition = case_when(
    condition == "diet_preferences" ~ "diet",
    condition == "personality_behaviors" ~ "personality",
    TRUE ~ condition) %>% 
      factor(levels = c("physical",
                        "diet",
                        "personality",
                        "heterogeneous",
                        "baseline"))) %>% 
  mutate(feature_type = case_when(
    feature_type == "diet_preferences" ~ "diet",
    feature_type == "personality_behaviors" ~ "personality",
    TRUE ~ feature_type) %>% 
      factor(levels = c("physical",
                        "diet",
                        "personality"))) %>% 
  # remove baseline condition
  filter(condition != "baseline")
```

```{r prevalence-vs-model}
# get average prevalence means
prevalence_means <- data_tidy %>% 
  group_by(condition, test_feature_type, test_feature) %>% 
  summarize(mean_prevalence = mean(prevalence)) 

# add column for model predictions
prevalence_vs_model <- prevalence_means %>% 
  left_join(model_test_feature_scores,
            by = c("condition", "test_feature_type" = "feature_type", "test_feature"))
```


```{r prevalence-vs-model-by-testfeaturetype-plot}
# get r^2 by test feature type
r2_by_condition <- prevalence_vs_model %>%
  group_by(test_feature_type) %>%
  summarise(
    r2 = summary(lm(mean_prevalence ~ score))$r.squared
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    label = paste0("r² = ", round(r2, 2)),
    # set annotation position (adjust if needed)
    x = 0.1, 
    y = 0.95
  )

# scatterplot mean prevalence vs model score by test feature type
prevalence_vs_model %>% 
  
  # make plot
  ggplot(aes(x = score,
             y = mean_prevalence,
             color = condition)) + 
  
  # facet by condition
  facet_wrap(vars(test_feature_type)) +
  
  # condition colors
  scale_color_manual(values = condition_colors) +
  
  # add data points
  geom_point() +
  
  # add regression line
  geom_smooth(method = "lm",
              se = TRUE,
              color = "black") +
  
  # customize axes
  scale_y_continuous(name = "mean rated prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "model score") +
  
  # add r^2 as annotation to each facet
  geom_text(data = r2_by_condition,
            aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
            color = "black",
            size = 4,
            hjust = 0) +
  
  # add title
  ggtitle("Mean prevalence vs model score by test feature type")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "prevalence_vs_model_score_by_testfeaturetype.png",
       height = 5,
       width = 7.5)
```

```{r prevalence-vs-model-by-condition-plot}
# get r^2 by condition
r2_by_condition <- prevalence_vs_model %>%
  group_by(condition) %>%
  summarise(
    r2 = summary(lm(mean_prevalence ~ score))$r.squared
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    label = paste0("r² = ", round(r2, 2)),
    # set annotation position (adjust if needed)
    x = 0.1, 
    y = 0.95
  )

# scatterplot mean prevalence vs model score by condition
prevalence_vs_model %>% 
  
  # make plot
  ggplot(aes(x = score,
             y = mean_prevalence,
             color = test_feature_type)) + 
  
  # facet by condition
  facet_wrap(vars(condition)) +
  
  # condition colors
  scale_color_manual(values = condition_colors) +
  
  # add data points
  geom_point() +
  
  # add regression line
  geom_smooth(method = "lm",
              se = TRUE,
              color = "black") +
  
  # customize axes
  scale_y_continuous(name = "mean rated prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  scale_x_continuous(name = "model score") +
  labs(color = "test feature type") +
  
  # add r^2 as annotation to each facet
  geom_text(data = r2_by_condition,
            aes(x = x, y = y, label = label),
            color = "black",
            size = 4,
            hjust = 0) +
  
  # add title
  ggtitle("Mean prevalence vs model score by condition")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "prevalence_vs_model_score_by_condition.png")
```


## Group characterization

Participants were asked to describe what characterizes Zarpies as a group, with responses coded by Marianna blind to condition. 

Eyeballing the plot below, participants in the diet and personality conditions often characterized Zarpies in terms of their diet or personality, seemingly moreso than in the other conditions. 

In the physical condition, participants appeared more likely to describe Zarpies in terms of physical characteristics than the other conditions, but this effect seems less pronounced than in the diet and personality conditions, with physical descriptions remaining a minority of descriptions in the physical condition. (maybe a bit more when merged with appearance, but still remaining below a majority) 

```{r plot-groupcharact}
# stacked bar plot of group_charact_coded by condition
data_tidy %>% 
  
  # summarize group_charact_coded
  group_by(condition) %>% 
  count(group_charact_coded) %>% 
  mutate(prop = n/sum(n)) %>% 

  # make plot
  ggplot(aes(y = n,
             x = condition,
             fill = group_charact_coded)) + 
  
  # add data points
  geom_bar(position = "fill",
           stat = "identity") +
  
  # customize axes
  theme(panel.border = element_blank(),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 6)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = NULL,
                     labels = scales::percent) +
  
  # add title
  ggtitle("Group characterization by condition",
          "What do you think characterizes Zarpies as a group?")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "group_charact_by_condition.png",
       height = 5,
       width = 10)
```

TBD: analyses of the frequency of these codes

# Secondary results


## Straight-lining

```{r straightlining}
# exact straightlining (50)
straightline_50_participants <-
  data_tidy %>% 
  group_by(participant) %>%
  filter(all(prevalence == 0.50)) %>% 
  distinct(participant)

# approximate straightlining (48-52)
straightline_approx_participants <-
  data_tidy %>% 
  group_by(participant) %>%
  filter(all(prevalence %in% seq(0.48, 0.52, 0.01))) %>% 
  distinct(participant)
```

Despite the induction task plots suggesting a lot of anchoring around the 50\% marker, straightlining was not a pervasive phenomenon. 

* `r straightline_50_participants %>% nrow()` out of `r sample$n` participants (`r label_percent(0.01)(nrow(straightline_50_participants)/sample$n)`) answered 50\% to all test questions. 

* `r straightline_approx_participants %>% nrow()` out of `r sample$n` participants (`r label_percent(0.01)(nrow(straightline_approx_participants)/sample$n)`) answered 48-52% to all test questions, a looser criterion.



## Test features order effects

All participants rated the prevalence of the same set of 15 test features, in random order. Did the order of test feature/prevalence judgment questions matter for prevalence judgments? 

```{r analysis-test-order-effects}
glmm_testfeatureorder <-
  glmmTMB(prevalence ~ condition * test_feature_type * test_feature_order + (1|participant) + (1|test_feature),
        data = data_tidy,
        family = beta_family(link = "logit"))

glmm_testfeatureorder %>% 
  Anova()

glmmTMB(prevalence ~ test_feature_order + (1|participant) + (1|test_feature),
        data = data_tidy,
        family = beta_family(link = "logit")) %>% 
  summary()
```
There is a main effect of test feature order on prevalence, but no significant interactions with condition, test feature type, or both. This suggests that while there may be some order effects in prevalence judgments, these effects do not differ by condition or test feature type.

```{r plot-induction-testfeatureorder}
# summary plot of prevalence by test feature order
data_tidy %>% 
  
  # make plot
  ggplot(aes(y = prevalence,
             x = test_feature_order)) + 
  
  # add data points
  geom_jitter(alpha = 0.01,
              position = position_jitter(width = .3)) +
  
  # add confidence intervals + means
  stat_summary(fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
               geom = "linerange",
               size = 1) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean,
               geom = "point",
               size = 2) +
  
  # customize axes
  theme(legend.title = element_text(size = 14),
        legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
        plot.caption = element_text(size = 8)) +
  scale_y_continuous(name = "prevalence",
                     limits = c(0, 1),
                     breaks = c(0, .25, .5, .75, 1), # labelled points on slider
                     labels = percent) +
  
  # remove legend
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  
  # add title
  ggtitle("Prevalence by test feature order")

ggsave(path = "figs",
       filename = "prevalence_by_testfeatureorder.png")
```


# Power analysis

```{r power-analysis-all, results = 'hide', echo = TRUE}
# set simulation parameters
set.seed(42) # reproducibility

n_sim <- # per sample size
  300

# total sample sizes
sample_sizes_to_test <- 
  c(
    50*4,
    75*4,
    100*4,
    125*4) 

# set criteria for power
alpha <- 0.05

# function to run a simulation
run_simulation <- function(sample_size_total){
  
  # identify _ participants in each condition to resample
  participants_to_resample <- bind_rows(
    data %>% 
      filter(condition == "physical") %>% 
      select(participant) %>% 
      slice_sample(n = sample_size_total/4,
                   replace = TRUE),
    data %>% 
      filter(condition == "diet") %>% 
      select(participant) %>% 
      slice_sample(n = sample_size_total/4,
                   replace = TRUE),
    data %>% 
      filter(condition == "personality") %>% 
      select(participant) %>% 
      slice_sample(n = sample_size_total/4,
                   replace = TRUE),
    data %>% 
      filter(condition == "heterogeneous") %>% 
      select(participant) %>% 
      slice_sample(n = sample_size_total/4,
                   replace = TRUE),
  ) 
  
  # resample data using list of participants to resample
  resampled_data <- data %>%
    inner_join(participants_to_resample,
               by = "participant") %>% 
    mutate(observation = row_number(),
           .before = everything())
  
  # tidy data
  resampled_data_tidy <- resampled_data %>% 
    
    pivot_longer(cols = diet_can_eat_spicy:phys_snore_sleep,
                 names_to = "test_feature_type_name",
                 values_to = "prevalence") %>% 
    
    # extract the order of test features (disregarding attention check)
    mutate(test_feature_order = map2_int(test_feature_type_name, test_features_order,
                                         function(feat, order){
                                           order_split <- str_split_1(order, '\\|') %>% # split test_features_order by |
                                             discard(\(x) x == "attn_check") # discard attention check
                                           match(feat, order_split)[1] # get first matching index
                                         })) %>%
    select(-test_features_order) %>% 
    
    # separate test feature type and abbv name
    separate(test_feature_type_name,
             into = c("test_feature_type", "test_feature_abbv"),
             extra = "merge") %>% 
    
    # clean feature type
    mutate(test_feature_type = case_when(
      test_feature_type == "phys" ~ "physical",
      test_feature_type == "diet" ~ "diet",
      test_feature_type == "pers" ~ "personality",
    ) %>% 
      factor(levels = c("physical",
                        "diet",
                        "personality"))) %>% 
    
    # get full feature name
    mutate(test_feature = test_feature_names[test_feature_abbv]) %>%
    
    # add match column between training and test feature type
    mutate(condition_test_match = 
             case_when(
               condition == "physical" & test_feature_type == "physical" ~ "match",
               condition == "diet" & test_feature_type == "diet" ~ "match",
               condition == "personality" & test_feature_type == "personality" ~ "match",
               condition == "heterogeneous" ~ "heterogeneous",
               TRUE ~ "mismatch"
             ) %>% 
             factor(levels = c("match", "heterogeneous", "mismatch"))) %>% 
    
    # add contrast columns for testing contrasts
    mutate(contrast_physical = if_else(condition == "physical", 3/4, -1/4),
           .after = condition) %>% 
    
    # add cosine similarity
    left_join(test_features_avg_cosine_similarities_by_condition,
              by = c("condition", "test_feature")) %>% 
    left_join(test_features_max_cosine_similarities_by_condition,
              by = c("condition", "test_feature"))
    
  
  
  # fit models & get p-values
  
  
  # average cosine similarity of the test feature, to the training features in that condition
  glmm_cosine_similarity_avg <-
    glmmTMB(prevalence ~ cosine_similarity_avg + (1|observation) + (1|test_feature), 
            data = resampled_data_tidy, 
            family = beta_family(link = "logit"))
  
  glmm_cosine_similarity_avg_p <-
    summary(glmm_cosine_similarity_avg)$coefficients$cond["cosine_similarity_avg", "Pr(>|z|)"]
  
  # match
  glmm_condition_test_match <-
    glmmTMB(prevalence ~ condition_test_match + (1|observation) + (1|test_feature), 
          data = resampled_data_tidy, 
          family = beta_family(link = "logit"))
  
  glmm_condition_test_match_p <- 
    glmm_condition_test_match %>% 
    emmeans(~ condition_test_match) %>% 
    contrast(method = "pairwise") %>% 
    summary() %>% 
    pull(p.value) %>% 
    .[2] # match vs mismatch
  
  
  # return p-values
  return(
    list("glmm_cosine_similarity_avg_p" = glmm_cosine_similarity_avg_p,
         "glmm_condition_test_match_p" = glmm_condition_test_match_p)
    )
}


# set up simulation grid with parameters
sim_grid <- 
  crossing(
    alpha = alpha,
    sample_size_total = sample_sizes_to_test, 
    simulation = rep(1:n_sim, length(sample_sizes_to_test)) # __ simulations per each possible sample size
  ) 

# run simulations for each row
sim_results <- sim_grid %>% 
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(
    vals = list(run_simulation(sample_size_total)),
    
    glmm_cosine_similarity_avg_p = vals$glmm_cosine_similarity_avg_p, 
    glmm_condition_test_match_p = vals$glmm_condition_test_match_p,
  ) %>%
  ungroup() 


# calculate power
power <- sim_results %>%
  group_by(sample_size_total) %>%
  summarize(
    glmm_cosine_similarity_avg = mean(glmm_cosine_similarity_avg_p < alpha),
    glmm_condition_test_match_p = mean(glmm_condition_test_match_p < alpha),
  ) 

# save results
write_csv(power, "study9_features_power_analysis.csv")
```

```{r power-results-all, echo=FALSE}
# pretty table
power %>% 
  gt() %>% 
  tab_header(
    title = "Power on target effects by total sample size",
    subtitle = "based on Study 9 data; total sample size across 4 conditions"
  ) %>%
  
  cols_label(
    sample_size_total = "total sample size",
    
    glmm_cosine_similarity_avg = "avg cosine similarity",
    glmm_condition_test_match_p = "match vs mismatch (ignore hetero)"
  )

```




# Footnotes

```{r}
sessionInfo()
```
